payload.title: Add cache-aware authorization execution without changing public APIs or adding dependencies.
payload.approach: Inject a cache-first path into AuthorizationService.authorize and only update it with version-stable snapshots when needed to preserve behavior and enable quick reversion.
payload.rationale: This keeps all external contracts intact while limiting work to cache lookup and snapshot-refresh logic that can be removed cleanly if regressions appear.

acceptanceCoverage.strategy: Call cache.get(subject-derived-key) before backend permission loading in authorize, and validate no permission check path changes when the cache returns a valid entry.
On cache miss or undefined snapshot, execute backend.version then backend.loadPermissions and persist the returned {version, permissions} snapshot with cache.set.
Add tests to confirm a non-stale cached snapshot yields authorization decisions without calling backend.loadPermissions for that request.
Add tests to confirm missing/outdated snapshot version triggers backend refresh and cache.set with refreshed snapshot data.
Retain existing assertions for allowed, denied, tenant isolation, invalid subjects, and backend failures to prove decision behavior is unchanged.
Extend test/authorization-service.test.ts to verify cache-hit, cache-miss, stale-refresh, cache-write behavior, and backend-call counts.

invariantProtection.strategy: Leave exported interfaces and method signatures unchanged, including constructor and authorize.
Do not add production dependencies or non-test tooling packages.
Preserve current tenant-separation and permission logic paths so existing authorization semantics remain identical.
Restrict edits to src/authorization-service.ts and test/authorization-service.test.ts only.
Keep TypeScript compile-time guarantees unchanged by using existing strict types and nullable-safe snapshot handling.

files.purpose: Implement cache-first/freshness logic within authorize while leaving all decision behavior and public surface intact.
Add deterministic cache-behavior tests for miss, hit, stale refresh, writeback, and tenant isolation with backend call-count checks.

steps.description: Map current authorize control flow and identify minimal insertion points for cache.get and freshness validation before backend permission loading.
Implement cache-first snapshot retrieval and stale check using backend.version as the freshness signal while retaining current validation and branching behavior.
Add miss/stale fallback path to load version and permissions from backend and persist via cache.set before permission evaluation.
Introduce test cases for cache miss, cache hit, stale snapshot refresh, and tenant isolation with explicit call-count assertions.
Reinforce regression tests for allowed/denied outcomes, invalid subjects, and backend errors to protect existing behavior.
Run npm test to verify build, type safety, and runtime behavior under the updated cache-aware authorization flow.

safetyTests.name: npm test
safetyTests.proves: Validates all acceptance criteria, invariants, and non-goals within the full configured build-and-run pipeline.

verificationCommands.name: npm test
verificationCommands.purpose: Run repository-required verification pipeline including compile and tests.

verificationCommands.name: npm run build
verificationCommands.purpose: Run an explicit TypeScript build check before final completion.

risks: Cache-key mismatch can break tenant isolation and authorization correctness.
Stale snapshot handling may serve outdated permissions if version comparisons are implemented incorrectly.
Over-eager cache hits can bypass required backend refresh for mutable permission sources.
Overly strict snapshot validation may introduce TypeScript or runtime edge-case failures under strict compiler flags.
assumptions: The cache key will remain based on the current subject context already used for tenant-bound permission evaluation.
Backend.version is the correct freshness check for authorization snapshots.
Testing can instrument backend and cache interactions under existing strict TypeScript constraints without adding libraries.

unknowns.description: Whether cache failures should be treated as ignorable misses or surfaced as hard errors is not currently defined.
unknowns.resolution: Default to soft-fallback-to-backend behavior and add a test documenting this choice, with no hard-fail change without explicit approval.

unknowns.description: Whether cache keys should be plain subject or include extra tenant normalization is not specified by existing code.
unknowns.resolution: Adopt plain subject-based keying initially and request approval before deviating.

unknowns.description: Whether cache.set failures should fail authorization or be treated as non-fatal is not specified.
unknowns.resolution: Treat cache.set failures as non-blocking for now and confirm any alternate policy before changing.

recovery: If acceptance criteria fail, revert only the cache branches in src/authorization-service.ts and keep fallback behavior identical while preserving test coverage.
If strict typing errors arise, tighten only local variable narrowing in authorization logic and restore unchanged interfaces.
If a cache test becomes brittle, simplify assertions to core call-order expectations and rerun npm test.
